title: SEGMENT 1 — The central dogma, and the virus that broke it

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — The central dogma, and the virus that broke it
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.
Today and for the next two hours, we're going to talk about retroviruses.

And I want to begin with something you already know, because the whole reason retroviruses are interesting starts with a piece of knowledge most of you learned in your first molecular biology course, and then learned, eventually, was wrong.
The central dogma.
You've all met it.
DNA makes RNA, RNA makes protein.
Francis Crick laid it out in 1957, refined it in 1970, and for about a decade it was taught as one of the cleanest, most satisfying organizing principles in all of biology.
Information flows from DNA to RNA to protein.
One direction.
A river, not a tide.
Now, retro — the word — means backward.
And retroviruses, the family we're about to spend two hours with, are called retroviruses because they go backward.
They take an RNA genome, and inside the cell they copy it into DNA.
RNA to DNA.
The wrong way down the river.
The enzyme that does this is called reverse transcriptase.
It is the namesake enzyme of the entire family, and the discovery of that enzyme in 1970 was a genuine paradigm shift — the kind of finding where the textbook had to be rewritten.
So that's the central conceptual surprise I want you to hold onto.
Forget everything else for a moment.
The defining feature of a retrovirus is that it carries, packaged inside the virion, an enzyme that breaks the central dogma.

Every other detail of retrovirus biology — the structure of the particle, the genome organization, the way these viruses enter cells, how they integrate into our chromosomes, how they cause disease, how we treat HIV, how we use HIV-derived vectors to do gene therapy in children with leukemia — every one of those details ultimately hangs off that one fact.
RNA goes in.
DNA comes out.
And then that DNA integrates into your genome, becoming, in a real and permanent sense, part of you.

If you walk out of this lecture in two hours and you can do three things — one, sketch the life cycle of a retrovirus from receptor binding to budding; two, walk a confused colleague through reverse transcription step by step and explain why the strand transfers happen; three, look at any antiretroviral drug and tell me what step of that cycle it interrupts — then we've succeeded.
Everything else is decoration on those three.
Let me also tell you why this matters beyond just being intellectually striking.
The retroviruses are a tiny family, taxonomically.
Maybe forty or so genera, depending on who's counting.
But they include the virus that caused, and is still causing, one of the most consequential pandemics in human history — HIV.
They include the only confirmed human oncoretroviruses, which gave us the first window into what an oncogene actually is.

They have left fossils inside our own DNA — about eight percent of your genome is the rotted-out remains of ancient retroviral infections, and one of those genes is essential for forming the placenta you developed inside.

And in the last two decades they've become the workhorse of gene therapy — when a child with severe combined immunodeficiency gets cured, when a CAR-T cell is engineered to hunt down a leukemia, an HIV-derived vector is doing the work.
So this is not a niche family.
This is a family that has been weaponized against us, that has been a tool we've turned against itself, and that is part of us in ways that earlier biology never anticipated.
Let's start at the beginning.
